responses: I live at 2240 W. 43rd street, Casper WY  82604  I have lived in this home for 19 years.  I have lived in Wyoming for 59 years and was born here in Casper.

I own my business Forefront Real Estate and have been a business owner for 10 years.  I have worked in various industries - oilfield, trucking, newspaper, and real estate.  I served on Casper City Council for 4 years and was Vice Mayor for a 1 1/2 years.  I am currently the Vice President of Wyoming Realtors, I served on Natrona County Historic Preservation Board, and I serve on Central Wyoming advocacy board for Central Wyoming Realtors.  I love our great state and want to preserve our values, support economic growth, and ensure future generations can enjoy the freedoms we cherish today.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is protecting our way of life while preparing for the future.  We are facing rising property taxes, housing shortages, rural healthcare gaps, education concerns, workforce shortages, and an economy that still depends heavily on energy and mineral revenue.  I believe our biggest opportunity is growth done the Wyoming way.  I support responsible energy development, including nuclear, Ai data centers, traditional energy, renewables, and new industries that bring good jobs while respecting water, property rights, and local communities.  I would make Wyoming more business-friendly, reduce unnecessary red tape, and keep taxes predictable so companies want to invest here.

Healthcare affordability and access are among the most important issues facing Wyoming families, especially in our rural communities.  No Wyoming resident should have to drive hours to see a specialist, wait months for care, or choose between paying for medication and paying their bills.  As a legislator. I would focus on practical solutions such as:  Strengthening rural healthcare, expand telehealth services, reduce healthcare costs through competition and transparency, support preventative care and wellness programs, and protect rural hospitals and clinics.  I believe healthcare decisions should remain as close to patients and their doctors as possible.  We need to remove barriers, encourage innovation, support local healthcare providers and ensure Wyoming families have access to affordable healthcare regardless of where they live.

I believe public lands are on of Wyoming's greatest assets and should be managed in a way that benefits the people of Wyoming.  I do not support large scale sales or transfers of public lands if they would reduce public access, harm Wyoming's outdoor heritage, or place additional financial burdens on taxpayers.  I believe we should have a strong voice in how our federal lands within our borders are managed.  We live, work, ranch, farm, hunt, recreate, and we raise families here, and our voice should be considered in Washington D.C.

Property tax relief is important because many Wyoming families, seniors, and small business owners are feeling the pressure of rising property values and rising tax bills.  People should not be taxed out of their homes simply because the market value of their property has increased.  The long-term solution is to continue to grow Wyoming's economy by attracting new businesses, supporting energy development, encourage innovation to create more jobs so we can broaden our tax base and reduce pressure on property owners over time.

The Wyoming Legislature has an important role in economic development but is not government's job to pick winners and losers in the marketplace.  Our role should be to create an environment where businesses, entrepreneurs, and workers can succeed.  We should create the opportunities so our children and grandchildren can build their futures in Wyoming instead of feeling they have to leave the state to find them.

My approach to campaign donations is simple:  transparency, integrity, and accountability.  Voters deserve to know who is supporting candidates and where campaign money is coming from.  Public trust in government depends on an open and transparent process.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
